perturbation.original_text: (f) "Arizona Assigned Trademarks" means the Trademarks set forth on Schedule 1.1(f) and all other Trademarks owned by Licensing or Seller as of the Effective Date and used or held for use exclusively in the in the Company Field as of the SPA Signing Date and/or as of the Effective Date (other than, for clarity any Arizona Licensed Trademarks).
perturbation.changed_text: (f) "Arizona Assigned Trademarks" means the Trademarks set forth on Schedule 1.1(f) and all other Marks owned by Licensing or Seller as of the Effective Date and used or held for use exclusively in the in the Company Field as of the SPA Signing Date and/or as of the Effective Date (other than, for clarity any Arizona Licensed Trademarks).
perturbation.explanation: The term **"Trademarks"** has been changed to **"Marks"** in the definition of **"Arizona Assigned Trademarks."** This introduces a perturbation because the contract consistently uses "Trademarks" elsewhere. This inconsistency leads to uncertainty regarding the scope of **"Arizona Assigned Trademarks"** and what is actually being assigned. This creates confusion since "Marks" is not previously defined, this can result in the Assigned Trademarks covering other Intellectual Properties (Trade Dress, etc) that were not intended to be assigned.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Assignment. Arizona agrees to assign and hereby assigns its entire right, title and interest in and to the Arizona Assigned IP to the Company.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the definition of "Arizona Assigned Trademarks" by replacing "Trademarks" with "Marks." This contradicts the "Assignment" section, which specifically refers to "Arizona Assigned IP" (intellectual property). Since "Marks" is not defined and could encompass broader intellectual property than intended, the assignment clause now potentially covers more than just Trademarks. This is a direct contradiction that would cause confusion because the contract previously only assigned trademarks and now might be assigning other IP. This falls under the 'Misaligned Terminology' definition because the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 Arizona Licensed Trademark Grant. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Arizona hereby grants to the Company a limited, non- exclusive, royalty-free, non-sublicensable (except as set forth in Section 7.1), non-assignable license in, to and under the Arizona Licensed Trademarks for the Arizona Trademark License Term for use in the Company Field throughout the world only in the form and manner that such Arizona Licensed Trademarks are used in the Business as of the Closing, provided that the Company shall use commercially reasonable efforts to present the Arizona Licensed Trademarks in the form set forth on Schedule 6.1.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 Arizona Licensed Trademark Grant. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Arizona hereby grants to the Company a limited, non- exclusive, royalty-free, non-sublicensable (except as set forth in Section 7.1), non-assignable license in, to and under the Arizona Licensed Marks for the Arizona Trademark License Term for use in the Company Field throughout the world only in the form and manner that such Arizona Licensed Trademarks are used in the Business as of the Closing, provided that the Company shall use commercially reasonable efforts to present the Arizona Licensed Trademarks in the form set forth on Schedule 6.1.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase **"Arizona Licensed Trademarks"** was changed to **"Arizona Licensed Marks".** This is a perturbation because it creates an inconsistency with the defined term and uses it interchangeably with **"Arizona Licensed Trademarks"** without defining it. This creates ambiguity about the scope of the licensed rights and obligations, as it is unclear whether all trademark rights or broader "mark" rights (which could include trade dress or other branding elements) are being licensed, making the obligations unclear.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.3 Quality Control. The Buyer Entities acknowledge the importance of Arizona's exercise of quality control over the use of the Seller Licensed Trademarks to preserve the continued integrity and validity of the Seller Licensed Trademarks and to protect the value and goodwill associated with the Seller Licensed Trademarks, and accordingly: (a) The Company shall ensure that all goods and services provided by the Company, under or in association with any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks, shall (i) be substantially the same as or greater than the quality of goods and services provided under such Seller Licensed Trademarks immediately prior to the Effective Date and (ii) not be associated with any goods or services, including any activities, that are reasonably likely to have an adverse effect on (A) the image or reputation of any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks or (B) Seller's right, title or interest in and to, any of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks.
perturbation.justification: The change from "Arizona Licensed Trademarks" to "Arizona Licensed Marks" introduces ambiguity regarding the scope of the licensed rights. The original text refers specifically to trademarks, while the changed text uses "Marks", which could encompass a broader range of branding elements. This contradicts section 6.3, which emphasizes quality control over the use of "Seller Licensed Trademarks" to maintain their integrity. If "Marks" include elements beyond trademarks, the quality control obligations become unclear, as the company's responsibility might extend beyond traditional trademark usage, creating an inconsistent obligation based on inconsistent terminology.

perturbation.original_text: 9.3 Enforcement. As between the Parties, Arizona, with respect to the Arizona Licensed IP, and the Company or Buyer, with respect to the Company Licensed IP, shall have the right (but not the obligation) to elect to bring a Proceeding or enter into settlement discussions regarding, or otherwise seek to resolve, any infringement, misappropriation, or other violation, or allegations of invalidity or unenforceability, of the Licensed IP. In the event that Arizona declines to institute any Proceedings against third-party infringers or violators of any Arizona Licensed Patents, regarding activities that would fall within the Company Field if conducted by the Company, within forty-five (45) days after being notified or becoming aware of such infringing conduct, the Company or Buyer shall have the right to institute any Proceedings against such third-party infringers or violators. In the event that the Company or Buyer elects to institute such Proceedings, Arizona will reasonably cooperate with the Company or Buyer in such Proceedings, and the Company or Buyer shall reimburse Arizona for all reasonable costs and fees incurred by Arizona as a result of such cooperation. Such cooperation by Arizona will include joining such Proceeding as a party, if deemed necessary by the Company or Buyer. In the event that Arizona elects to bring a Proceeding against any alleged infringer of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks and seeks the cooperation of the Licensor of the Trademark License Agreement in such Proceeding, Arizona will take reasonable steps to assist the Company or Buyer in requesting the cooperation of the Licensor of the Trademark License Agreement, and pursuing an infringement claim against such alleged infringer. The Company or Buyer, as applicable, shall retain all benefits, recoveries, injunctions or other value derived from such Proceedings instituted by such Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.3 Enforcement. As between the Parties, Arizona, with respect to the Arizona Licensed IP, and the Company or Buyer, with respect to the Company Licensed IP, shall have the right (but not the obligation) to elect to bring a Proceeding or enter into settlement discussions regarding, or otherwise seek to resolve, any infringement, misappropriation, or other violation, or allegations of invalidity or unenforceability, of the Licensed IP. In the event that Arizona declines to institute any Proceedings against third-party infringers or violators of any Arizona Licensed Patents, regarding activities that would fall within the Company Field if conducted by the Company, within forty-five (45) days after being notified or becoming aware of such infringing conduct, the Company or Buyer shall have the right to institute any Proceedings against such third-party infringers or violators. In the event that the Company or Buyer elects to institute such Proceedings, Arizona will reasonably cooperate with the Company or Buyer in such Proceedings, and the Company or Buyer shall reimburse Arizona for all reasonable costs and fees incurred by Arizona as a result of such cooperation. Such cooperation by Arizona will include joining such Proceeding as a party, if deemed necessary by the Company or Buyer. In the event that Arizona elects to bring a Proceeding against any alleged infringer of the Arizona Licensed Marks and seeks the cooperation of the Licensor of the Trademark License Agreement in such Proceeding, Arizona will take reasonable steps to assist the Company or Buyer in requesting the cooperation of the Licensor of the Trademark License Agreement, and pursuing an infringement claim against such alleged infringer. The Company or Buyer, as applicable, shall retain all benefits, recoveries, injunctions or other value derived from such Proceedings instituted by such Party.
perturbation.explanation: The term **"Arizona Licensed Trademarks"** was changed to **"Arizona Licensed Marks"** in the enforcement clause. This creates uncertainty, since Arizona Licensed Marks, isn't defined. While "Arizona Licensed Trademarks" refers specifically to registered and unregistered trademarks licensed to the Company, "Arizona Licensed Marks" is undefined, and therefore can be used to argue covers broader branding elements. The change affects how enforcement actions are pursued and what constitutes infringement, since the scope of what Arizona is obligated to enforce on behalf of the Company is left ambiguous, by not refering to Trademarks.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9.1 Responsibility and Cooperation. As between the Parties, Arizona, with respect to the Arizona Licensed IP, and the Buyer Entities, with respect to the Company Licensed IP, shall have the right (but not the obligation) for filing, prosecuting, and maintaining all Arizona Licensed IP and Company Licensed IP, respectively, in the licensing Party's name. For the avoidance of doubt, in case either such Party files any new Intellectual Property registration to the extent covering the Licensed IP, such new Intellectual Property registration shall automatically become Licensed IP. However, and for the further avoidance of doubt, the aforementioned shall not apply to new Intellectual Property created by a licensee Party or its Sublicensees separately and independently from the Licensed IP, for example in case of separate and independent technical enhancements or advancements. The Parties shall reasonably consult and coordinate with each other at the other Party's request with respect to the matters set forth in this Section 9.1.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies "Arizona Licensed Trademarks" to "Arizona Licensed Marks." This introduces ambiguity because "Arizona Licensed Marks" is undefined, while the original term clearly refers to registered and unregistered trademarks. This creates a strong contradiction with Section 9.1, which outlines responsibility and cooperation regarding "Arizona Licensed IP." Since "Arizona Licensed IP" is likely defined elsewhere to include only trademarks, the change now creates ambiguity about the scope of Arizona's responsibility regarding intellectual property beyond trademarks, leading to uncertainty in enforcement and conflicting obligations.
